summary: Gruber chronicles a rapid-fire series of escalating Twitter controversies under Elon Musk's ownership. He begins with Jack Dorsey's ill-timed newsletter launch on Twitter's own Revue platform, which was shut down within 24 hours, then catalogs Musk's banning of journalists, the disabling of Twitter Spaces after Musk was confronted live, the blocking of all Mastodon links as 'malware,' and the banning of Mastodon's official account. Gruber frames these actions as far beyond hypocrisy, calling them raw petulance from someone who treats Twitter as a personal $44 billion playground. He closes by noting the absurdity of Musk seeking new investors at full price while the platform's value craters, and warns that Musk's erratic behavior is damaging Tesla as well.
key_points: Jack Dorsey's principle that only original authors should remove content reveals a dangerous naivety about moderation that partly explains Twitter's trajectory
Musk's banning of journalists and suppression of Twitter Spaces after being confronted live demonstrates content moderation under his regime is pure petulance, not principle
Twitter classifying all Mastodon links as 'malware' and banning Mastodon's official account exposes the hollowness of Musk's free speech claims
Musk seeking investors at $54.20/share while actively destroying the platform's value and revenue is a nearly impossible sell
Musk's very public unraveling is dragging down Tesla's stock and brand, threatening the hype-driven valuation that sustains the company
money_quotes: Content moderation at Twitter under Musk's regime is simply raw, unadulterated petulance.
He clearly sees the entirety of Twitter as his own personal $44 billion playground and a vicious cudgel to be wielded against his perceived enemies.
That the man who led Twitter from 2015–2021 seemingly lacks that imagination or understanding at least partially explains how we got here.
Reneging on that promise is hypocrisy. Suspending Twitter users who had the temerity to simply post links to the ElonJet accounts on Instagram and Mastodon is beyond hypocrisy. They were just links.
It'd be bad enough if they banned links to Mastodon accounts as part of a new policy disallowing links to Twitter competitors, but Twitter is flat-out lying that Mastodon is in any way dangerous.
Selling shares of Twitter at $54.20 today could be grist for a sequel to Glengarry Glen Ross.

It seems clear that many people who heretofore bought into Tesla because they bought into Musk's hype are coming to the realization that he's an erratic huckster, a liar, and a low-grade (at least) sociopath.
Other than all that, though, things are going just great at Twitter.
key_insight: Musk's supposed free speech absolutism has been revealed as nothing more than personal whim, with his erratic and petulant content moderation decisions threatening to destroy both Twitter and his broader business empire.
